svg.title: Q1 end-to-end stack — heterogeneous transmon ↔ Diamond-SiV link, layer by layer
svg.desc: A seven-layer stack diagram answering LOI Q1: what's required, from the compute layer down, to move quantum information between two QCs. Node A (superconducting transmon, microwave-native) on the left column, Node B (Diamond-SiV, visible-native) on the right. Each layer is a horizontal band: Application (data qubit ψ at each end); Logical/QEC (surface-code patches at the endpoints — EC lives at the endpoints, not in the channel); Network service (the abstraction "Bell pair on demand"); Distillation (raw pairs purified to high fidelity); Link/swap (1G repeater chain with three SiV relays and four photonic BSMs, abbreviated); Encoding + bridges (M-O transduction at A, QFC at B and relays, time-bin encoding on the fibre); Physical channel (telecom O-band photons in fibre, ~0.3 dB/km). The asymmetry is the point: A's microwave matter qubit needs M-O transduction (current SOTA <1%); B's 737 nm SiV qubit needs QFC (50–80% mature). Wavelengths grounded in Knaut et al. Nature 629.573 (2024).
svg.text: Q1 stack — heterogeneous transmon ↔ Diamond-SiV link, layer by layer
what's required from the compute layer down · wavelengths from Knaut et al. Nature 629.573 (2024)
data qubit at ~5 GHz microwave · in a dilution fridge
data + comm qubit at 737 nm (visible) · cryogenic
share / teleport |ψ⟩ between A and B
EC protects the endpoints' data qubits.
The channel itself is not error-corrected
— it relies on purification (L4) and heralding.
teleport, distribute keys, run distributed protocols
all photons on the link at 1350 nm telecom O-band
single-mode fibre · Knaut 35 km deployed Boston-metro · 17 dB loss
SNSPDs at receivers · ~2.5 Hz dark counts
Knaut et al. Nature 629.573 (2024) for wavelengths and 35 km deployed-fibre demo · Awschalom et al. PRX Quantum 2.017002 (2021) for QuIC stack framing
QEC + purification live at endpoints (L4, L6) · bridges (L2) appear only where adjacent layers mismatch in wavelength or modality · no QFC/M-O on the middle fibre — already at telecom
